speaker: interviewer
text: Give me thirty seconds.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I think I can give you thirty seconds. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] So you had known--

speaker: interviewee
text: So you think I'm doing well?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're doing great!

speaker: interviewer
text: You're doing great. I want to know about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm going to continue.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I need to know--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is tape number four, on Thursday August 11th, 1994, with Sol Liber, L-I-B-E-R. And we're in Beverly Hills, California, and my name is Meryl Goldberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was hiding under the barrack, and I came across wrapped bread and a half in a big handkerchief, like women used to wear on their heads, you know? I figure, if I'm sitting here, might as well have a meal. I ate up half a bread, and we went to sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next morning, this guy was walking around like a poison rat, you know? He wanted to find out who-- who took this bread. You see, it's very hard to steal from a thief, you know. He's very un-- very upset. And we walked around for a-- another few days, breaking in those shoes.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hakiva Leifer talked to the people inside. He took me into the [NON-ENGLISH]. I cleaned the pots that they delivered the food and I put up-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I straightened out the straw sacks against the wall when the people left. Well I was-- at least I didn't have to go march again. But one Sunday, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as I'm putting the straw sacks, stack them up against the wall, they were going up against-- so high against the roof, against the ceiling inside, because there were a couple hundred people in the-- in the barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: An SS man comes in with a new whip. You could see it's new. It's not broken in yet. And drunk. And the foreman of the barracks says-- they used to call out, achtung! You know what achtung means? OK. Everybody stood up. I couldn't stand up, because I was right against the ceiling. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He calls me down, [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH]? I say, yes. [NON-ENGLISH] but the-- I couldn't get up, because I was against the ceiling. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: He took me down. He told the-- the one guy from the barrack. He put my head between his knees, and he gave me 25. With his new-- he broke in the new-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Whip.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I see for one thing if I-- the new--

speaker: interviewer
text: New whip.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whip. OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got a beating like that and I couldn't sit down for weeks. This Hakiva Leifer brought me some cold towels, and I applied to it. You'll excuse my expression. My behind was cut like with a knife. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after a couple of weeks, I improved, but still no sitting. I was standing up continuously. They came from Skarzysko to pick up a few hundred people to the ammunition factory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] See, I have to back up. I have to back up because when I came-- I was-- I have to back up and come back to ghetto, if you don't mind. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] While we were fighting, we got orders to take out young people to the forest, to ship out. We shipped them in the-- they went through the sewer. And on the other side, people were waiting, took them away. The first shipment was 40 young people, girls and boys. The second-- the second shipment, they-- somebody squealed. Either from the other side-- I suppose from the other side. And they were waiting for us. First they put in tear gas. If you make it in there, tear gas mixing with the sewer stuff-- nausea. We were caught in crossfire. I-- I was-- I was wounded twice. Once, I still have a mark on the skin, like a burn on the skin. And the other one I was in my-- got my shoulder. About 10 people died, and we all backed out. We opened fire also, and about 10 people died.

speaker: interviewee
text: And now I'm going to come back to Majdanek. When I came into Majdanek, I had my bullet. It must have come from far away. It was stuck in my shoulder. And I asked if there are any doctors. They say, doctors? Butchers.

speaker: interviewee
text: They called and two guys came in. They told me to lie down on the table and move-- move out when my-- where the bullet was, to move out of the table. They-- I don't know what they cut it, a razor blade or a knife. They cut it open and the lead fell in the glass. But it was already infected, you know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They put in some gauze. They washed it out, put in some gauze. And the next day they came and started pulling it out. It-- it was hurting like hell, you know? It was dry. But they did the best they could. They cleaned it out again. They put a band-aid about two inches wide on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I'm coming back to going to Skarzysko. They picked the people to go to Skarzysko and I-- I went in line. I said, anything will be better than here. And they told us we were going to an ammunition factory to work. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I stayed in line, and they used to have the SA and SS. The SA was Hitler's first support group. He was-- they were the SA. A tall guy, about seven feet. His name was Zeidel. I still remember the name. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Everybody stays in line to go through. The doctor told you to undress to the waistline. And I put my-- I was afraid. I'm a wounded man. I cannot pass the selection. They called it selection. I put my shirt on my shoulder.

speaker: interviewee
text: He comes over to me. He says [GERMAN]. [GERMAN] is like a bad person. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He already caught me. I take off the shirt, that's all. Was ist das? So I told him. They picked me out-- they picked a group out to go load ammunition to the front line, and-- in crates. And a crate fell on me and I scratched my shoulder. [GERMAN]? I said, if I would come to the doctor, they would ask me, I would tell them. I'm not-- I'm not in line at the doctor's place yet. He takes me by the shoulder, takes me over to the doctor. He says, pass him. Passed me so, and-- OK. I was really lucky. I passed.

speaker: interviewee
text: The rest was left, right, left, right. If you went left, you were in the gas chamber. We-- they put us-- the same day they gave us a double portion of bread, and they put us in the cars and we traveled a half a day and a night. Also pretty crowded.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my shoulder was pinching, you know? It was-- the job wasn't done right. They did the best they can without any medication. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to the station at Skarzysko. Five people worked at this station, fixing the rails. And I saw two boys, two brothers from my city. They told me, Schlomo, see that you go on Werk A. There's A, B, and C. They used to call it A, B, and C. If you're learning French, you must know it's a little bit different than English.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, what choice do I have? I cannot ask where to go. He says, well, try. This guy comes over. This Zeidel comes over. It's funny. I don't know until today what he saw in me. He comes over, brings me like a trough for a horse. [GERMAN]. For food. A metal can, it was black.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, [GERMAN]. I didn't know what a [GERMAN] is. It's a place where you harden steel to push out the shells for the howitzers. I asked the guys there. They were coming closer a little bit. They were without the guard-- five people.

speaker: interviewee
text: What'd he say? What'd he say? I tell him I'm going to work on the howitzer. He says, good, that's good. It's in Werk A. The other people, they went in B. They used to work on-- they called it [GERMAN]. It's a powder to put in the shells, that you get yellow inside and outside in two days.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Polish people used to get masks, they worked with masks. The Jews didn't anything. So when it came to-- the people that came with us, came for-- they didn't have showers. They had to come to us. It was two kilometers from the other Werk. When they came, we couldn't believe it. There were all yellow, like canaries. And when they tell you-- if somebody told you he's going to die tonight, he didn't live until the morning. They knew that they're going to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, came into this-- they had barracks across from the-- from the ammunition factory. I met a lot of people from my city, a lot. How many? 20, 30. I call that a lot because I didn't see so many in a long time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we slept on bunks, four bunks up. High. I was assigned a bunk, you know, and I was asking around about the situation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a working camp. It's not a death camp, but you have to be careful what you do. Next day, they took me to work, showed me what I'm going to do. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every piece of steel came with a sheet that told you how high the temperature should be and on what-- in what kind of material it should be cooled. You cool it in oil, you cool it in saltwater, or [INAUDIBLE] wax.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you took it on a chain. You took it to the proper place. So I read it, and after a day I went to work. You wear a glove till here. Long gloves up to the shoulders because it was 1,300 degrees in the oven.

speaker: interviewee
text: When you opened it, you couldn't-- and you had to be careful, because if it took a second longer when the temperature came to about, then it formed a skin and it wasn't good anymore. Just a little skin, it was-- wasn't good. Has to be precise. And I worked a few weeks, and the food was lousy. Lousy. And the Polish people used to get better soup with meat. And in the wartime, every one of those people had a little ground around the houses where they lived. They used to get up very early and plant some-- all kinds of potatoes. Who knows what they planted. Their food for the winter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, my work, I put in, let's say, two, three pieces. The oven had to wait a half an hour to really heat it up, and I had plenty of time. There were three ovens. I say, why don't you let me work for you? You'll bring me some bread and I'll work for you. I could do your work and you'll sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Meister will come in, the boss they will come in, I'll wake you up. I'll give you a signal. He says fine. I did work for three. Everybody brought me some bread and they gave me their soup. This kept me on my feet. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And-- but people were falling. One day, we walked home from the night shift. There was a-- like a hunchback. See, the Germans, they couldn't go on the front line if they weren't fit. They gave them jobs in camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he stops the whole line, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and he says, Liber, [GERMAN]. And if you were called out, you know it's not a good sign. Takes a chair and he puts me in a chair. I say, what did I do? [GERMAN]? [GERMAN]. [SIGHS] Puts me on the chair, and turns and tells everybody to turn around. He says, why-- [NON-ENGLISH]? He says, why is Liber so clean? He didn't know why. I knew. They were-- the people were working at machines that spilled a lot of oil, and they had no-- there was no double clothes. That's what you had. That's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody stays and he yells, you know, sort of. And I didn't want to tell anything. I thank God that's the only thing he's going to do, is show them how clean I am. Because the SS used to come and take away beautiful girls and never showed up again. They never came back. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I-- then he took me down. He said-- he told the people to go into camp. He asked me why. I say, when I come to work, I have the-- the occasion-- I have hot water and I have a powder that we use to clean the things in the-- [INAUDIBLE] dry. And whenever I come in, I wash my clothes. I work night shift. In the morning, it dries out at the oven. It's so hot. That's why. I say-- I say, [GERMAN]. They haven't got the same situation I have. But still, he says, they could keep a little bit cleaner. There's-- there is water in the camp here. They could wash. Some-- some neglected themselves. It was the worst thing, if you went down with cleanliness. They hate it. Anyway, after this I worked for-- in [INAUDIBLE] for a long time. And-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] and until we had to-- the Russians came 10 kilometers from this factory. And they said, it came on order, it's like a tornado hit them. To pack everything, pack the machines. We are moving out to [? Czechow ?]. [? Czechow? ?] How far would it take the Russians to come to [? Czechow? ?] But they tried. They didn't believe that the end is coming. OK, we packed everything. We packed in. And then the Russians came closer, they said [GERMAN]. They put us in trains and they shipped us out to [? Czechow. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We came to [? Czechow. ?] There's an empty-- empty-- empty lofts. Nothing. Nowhere to sleep. They put some straw on the cement, where we sleep. There were three places in [? Czechow ?] from the same factory, ammunition factory. There were old factories that they put people in to fill it in, in two factories. Our people they brought in. We had to build a new one. They brought in a machine to build up a new factory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, to them, food was the least they were worried about for us. They could always supply other people, you know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We start putting in the machines, brought in. And he comes over to me-- there was a-- a boss. Called him a Meister. Praucher was his name. See how I remember 50 years?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they brought in-- from Skarzysko, they brought in a diesel locomotive that took the cars to the station, to the factory, emptied out, and brought full-- full load again. And there was a crane-- not like we have the cranes here now-- a crane that went across the-- the rails, and that loaded-- if you had to unload--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a factory that made materials. And if they had to unload, there was a hook you could move back and forth, unload. And he says to me, he make me for a partner. [GERMAN] He takes me-- what will we do, you know? Like I would own a machine, you know? The crane could take down, let's say, a ton. The locomotive weighed two ton. [INAUDIBLE]. I said, you know what, Mr. Praucher? If you're not going to shoot me if this crane breaks, I'll take it on my own. He says, [GERMAN]. He says, do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have no way out, we have to unload and we have so many cars waiting to unload. I say, give me four people, and he gives me four people. I'll pick the people. HE picked four people. I say, you go in the front. If I raise the locomotive two, three inches, you pull the flatcar away, and I'm going to lower it. We pick up the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the locomotive. It hangs sideways. They didn't connect the-- the-- the chains right. So we lower it again to the flatcar and we straighten it out a little bit. I told them how to do it. I pick this locomotive up, and the crane starts squeaking. I figure, any minute now. There was a wheel I turned slowly up to pick it up. And I picked it up about two, three inches, and I say, you guys pull it. I-- this guy was sweating more than me. And I let down the locomotive and I told him to push it in the right place to lower it on the-- on the rail. We put it down. From that day on, he brought me, every day, two slices of bread, about an inch thick. Two slices. And a couple cigarettes. And we start bringing in-- the guys that worked in Skarzysko on this locomotive were there, and they right away went on this locomotive to start switching the cars. Bringing in loaded cars to unload and the empties out to the station back. And we worked there on this, but the-- I worked mostly on the outside. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we worked there for quite a while, until about 194-- end of 1944, beginning '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the same thing happened like in Skarzysko. How long does it take to go through Poland? If you go forward, just like the German went in-- the Germans went into Russia. When you go in with power, it's moving ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- we could hear, the Russians had they called [? katyushas. ?] Like a artillery that you-- like missiles. You put them in tubes, like about 24, but they were whistling like a terrible noise they made, that you-- you get scared before they hit the ground. And we were-- we were already encountering these missiles coming into the city. And the same thing-- raus, raus. Schnell. As they-- the SS were watching us, the Ukrainians, you could see Germans surrounding the locomotives, hanging onto everything they could hold onto, running. They put us in the-- in cars. First they promised us they were going to take us to another factory. But when we came to the other factory, there was one SS man and his name was Batenschlager. He was standing at the front door and says, [GERMAN] to the train. [GERMAN] to the-- straight to the train. We came to the train. They were crying as we're walking around. They were afraid for the-- of Russians, you know, because they were countrymen. They put us in there and we stayed until about 1:00 in the morning, loaded into the trains. And about 1:00, the train took off for Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Buchenwald. First of all, they picked up the police that were the real-- the real murderers, you know? And they disappeared. You didn't see them anymore. And there were so many people. They were bringing in people from all over. From all the Polish camps, you know, and from Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we-- they assigned us to the barrack 52. And 12 people to a bunk. If you wanted to turn around, everybody had to turn around. And people were dying like flies. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One morning-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you went out to the-- to the toilet, they gave you two seconds. When you went to wash your face, you had no towel. You wiped yourself with a sleeve, but before-- the sleeve froze. It got stiff from the water. That's how cold it was. We were staying back to back to keep warm, if we stood in line for food or whatever, to go to the bathroom. One day, somebody comes in. They say they need a couple hundred people. They called it commando to go out of Buchenwald. I said, I'm the first one out of here. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us, about a couple hundred people, and we-- we traveled the whole day. We came in. It was dark already. It looked like Siberia. The snow-- you just saw barracks and snow, just like Siberia. What was the work there? They stole all the fac-- all the machinery from all over Europe. They didn't-- they were--

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll have to backtrack here a little bit. I hope the people that see it someday will forgive me. When we came to [? Czechow ?], all the guards from all the camps had nothing to do. So we had to unload the things, some steel, I think. They were going around with dogs and hitting.

speaker: interviewer
text: You picked up heavy things that you wouldn't be able to move it today. 10 people wouldn't be able to move it. And that's why we talked about dogs. They used to tear pieces out of you. They just told them to go and get you, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, I'll come back now on the tape where I was going. They took us out of Buchenwald. We came in at night to this camp, and I hear speaking French. And I-- to me, French was a foreign-- foreign thing altogether in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked some people there, what language do they speak? He says-- there was a German kapo. He was a communist. He was in-- in the camps for years. He says, [GERMAN]. They are French people. And most of the people working in the kitchen were all French.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we came to this camp, they gave me some fresh clothes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- something was like paper cracking in my pocket. The pocket has a-- always a piece of material on the end. And some people went, probably, to Auschwitz, to the gas chamber, they'd sew in the pants-- maybe somebody sewed in some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took out about 10,000 francs. I didn't know what kind of francs they were. So I went to the kitchen and I said to the kitchen people, you're a Frenchman. Someday you'll go home. You'll have money. He says, but this money is not our money. This is Vichy money. You know, it was the government in Vichy. So I said, what do you care? The only thing you have to give me is some more soup. It's not going to hurt you. I'll give you the money. So I gave him the money and he supplied some food for about a month. He gave me some more food. I figured every little bit helps. Next day they took us to work. They took us down to this salt mine. The stench, you know, of the mine, and that. And we had new bosses, new Germans there. And what are we going to do here? He says we have to take down machinery.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were coming, machinery from Europe, from all over the-- into Germany. They stole all the machinery. But the salt mines didn't have big enough elevators to bring it down, so we had to put them on, straight up, the machinery. Every machine got up in 10, 12 feet. We had to put them up straight, take them down. And there was no work like today. We had to roll them on tubes, for miles. We pushed them, and then you took from the front the tube, from the back the tube, and put in the front again, and that's how-- and we worked there for months. And then they-- they-- the Allies bombed the-- the factories that made ball bearings, because this was for-- for the war, for planes, for tanks. I think it was Schweinfurt, if I'm not mistaken. They bombed all the-- so they brought in the machinery to the salt mine. They put me to work to make the little balls. There was one machine that cut precisely, and the other one made it round. It's like-- there was a machine that round it up. Then it went to another place that put it in the ball bearings. They put it in grease and put it in. There were women sitting-- Ukrainian women. And that's how I worked for a while. And one day, the-- when I came in-- we came in from Poland, we all looked good, because you were, like, almost at home. You helped yourself. In [? Czechow, ?] I used to work for the factory that made the material. We used to get some Polish soup that they serve to their own.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came in really not-- not skinny, you know, like we became after. The boss comes over to me and says, Liber, [GERMAN]. Every day-- I'm going to say it in English-- every day you're getting skinnier. I was afraid to say something. You know, in those days I were so nervous. Shooting a person was like a fly on the wall. I say, what the heck. He's asking me. I'm going to tell him. I say, we're not getting enough food. He says, yes, but we don't-- we're not getting enough food either. It's a war going on. I say, you asked me. I gave you an answer. So he used to-- That's it? [INAUDIBLE]
